title: Comment on Trump Executive Actions on Climate Change, March 28, 2017
summary: President Trump's executive actions of today, which attempt to undermine progress made under President Obama to combat the menace of human-caused climate change, are yet another example of the new administration's propensity to let blind ideology "trump" clear-eyed science and good sense.
body: President Trump's executive actions of today, which attempt to undermine progress made under President Obama to combat the menace of human-caused climate change, are yet another example of the new administration's propensity to let blind ideology "trump" clear-eyed science and good sense. It is clear beyond reasonable scientific doubt that: (1) climate change is occurring globally at a pace and in a pattern not explained by natural influences; (2) the pace and pattern are explained by human emissions from fossil-fuel burning and land-use change; (3) the ongoing changes in climate are already causing serious harm to human health, property, and livelihoods from increases in heatwaves, wildfires, pest outbreaks, torrential downpours, and the most powerful storms, as well as sea-level rise and shifts in the abundance and distribution of species (those we need, those we love, and those we hate); and (4) future harm in these categories will be far less if the world continues to act collectively to reduce the offending emissions than if it does not. To ignore these realities, as the Trump administration is now doing, not only will slow global progress on preventing a truly catastrophic degree of climate change; it also will unilaterally surrender the leadership position the United States has enjoyed, under President Obama, in the global effort to meet this common challenge; and, by abandoning the U.S. Government's efforts to support development and implementation of the most cost-effective remedies, it will ultimately sacrifice this country's global economic competitiveness as well. John P. Holdren Teresa and John Heinz Professor of Environmental Policy John F. Kennedy School of Government Harvard University And Senior Advisor to the President The Woods Hole Research Center The latest from Belfer See more Statements and views expressed in this commentary are solely those of the author and do not imply endorsement by Harvard University, the Harvard Kennedy School, or the Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs. Recommended citation Holdren, John P. “Comment on Trump Executive Actions on Climate Change, March 28, 2017.” Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs, Harvard Kennedy School. _list.source: Belfer Center for Science and International Affairs, Harvard Kennedy School

result.main_content_en: The article criticizes President Trump's executive actions of March 28, 2017, for undermining Obama-era climate policies. It asserts, based on scientific consensus, that climate change is human-caused, driven by fossil fuel emissions and land-use changes, and is already causing significant harm through extreme weather, sea-level rise, and ecological shifts. The author argues that ignoring these realities will slow global progress, cause the U.S. to surrender its international leadership on climate action, and ultimately harm the nation's economic competitiveness by abandoning cost-effective solutions and technological development.
result.main_content_zh: 本文批评特朗普总统于2017年3月28日发布的行政命令，认为这些行动破坏了奥巴马时期的气候政策。文章基于科学共识指出，气候变化由人类活动导致，主要源于化石燃料燃烧和土地利用变化，并已通过极端天气、海平面上升和生态分布变化造成严重危害。作者认为，无视这些现实将减缓全球防止气候灾难的进程，导致美国放弃其在应对这一共同挑战中的国际领导地位，同时，由于放弃了支持最具成本效益的解决方案和技术发展的政府努力，最终也将损害美国的经济竞争力。
